nameMeanings.name: Walter
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin), German, Dutch, Polish, Swedish, and Danish: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements wald ‘rule’ + heri, hari ‘army’. This personal name was introduced into England both in the reign of Edward the Confessor and by the Normans (in the forms Walt(i)er, Waut(i)er). After the Norman Conquest it soon became one of the most popular personal names in Britain. Compare Walther .
Germanized or Americanized form of Czech, Slovak, Croatian, and Slovenian Valter and Czech Valtr: from the personal name Valter, Valtr, of ancient Germanic origin (see 1 above).

variants.name: Walthers
meanings: German: patronymic from the personal name Walther (see Walter ).

variants.name: McQuate
meanings: Scottish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Uait ‘son of Uat’, Gaelic form of the personal name Wat, a short form of Walter . Compare the Irish form McQuaid .

variants.name: Waltke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Jurgen, Wilhelmina.
German: from a pet form of any of the ancient Germanic personal names formed with Old High German waltan ‘to rule’ as the first element, as in Walter .

variants.name: McWaters
meanings: Scottish and northern Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Uaitéir, a patronymic from a Gaelicized form of the personal name Walter , with excrescent English -s.

variants.name: Wahler
meanings: German:
from the personal name Wol(d)er, Low German form of Walter .
habitational name for someone from any of various places called Wahl, Wahle, or Wahlen.

variants.name: McWalter
meanings: Irish and Scottish: from Gaelic Mac Uaitéir ‘son of Walter ’.

variants.name: Walters
meanings: English: variant of Walter , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.
German: patronymic from   Walter  .

variants.name: McQuaid
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Colum, Nuala.
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Uaid ‘son of Uad’, Gaelic form of the personal name Wat, a short form of Walter . Compare the Scottish form McQuate .

variants.name: Walther
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Hans, Erwin, Wolfgang, Otto, Gerhard, Gunter, Heinz, Klaus, Konrad, Bernd, Bernhard.
German, Dutch, Swedish, and Danish: variant of   Walter  .

variants.name: Lauter
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Ernst, Erwin, Kurt, Sigismund, Wolf.

German: topographic name and habitational name, from a common stream name meaning ‘clear’, from Middle High German hlutra, or from any of the places called Lauter or Lauta (Saxony). This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine) and some other European countries.
Germanized or Americanized form of Slovenian Lavtar or its Croatized and rare Slovenian variant Lautar: from a dialect variant of the personal name Valter (see Walter ).

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

en.lifeSummary: Richard Walter married Elizabeth before 1562, in Suffolk, England. He lived in Worstead, Norfolk, England in 1577. He died before 4 June 1569, in Nayland, Suffolk, England, and was buried in Nayland, Suffolk, England.
